items.prompt: For research-facing reproducibility, documentation should primarily record:
choices: The exact analysis conditions: dataset version, transform order, and known limitations
Only the marketing slogan for the repository brand
Only the color theme of the lecture slides
Only CAP consistency trade-offs for distributed stores
items.explain: Section 13.2 emphasizes recording dataset version, transformation order, and limitations that affect conclusions.

items.prompt: Publication-grade repository metadata mainly exists so other researchers can discover and reuse a dataset beyond its original study.
items.explain: Example 13.10 shows standards-compliant metadata enabling discovery in a repository.

items.prompt: What goes wrong when a published analysis does not pin the dataset version?
choices: Later updates can silently change the data against which replication is attempted
FAIR Findable becomes automatic without identifiers
HIPAA automatically converts to an unrestricted open license
items.explain: Example 13.11 shows that unpinned versions make the original analysis conditions unrecoverable.

items.prompt: Name one metadata field that helps repository discovery in Example 13.10.
items.rubric: Accept title, keywords, methodology, license, or equivalent standards-compliant fields from the example.

items.prompt: This part treats Chapter 8 documentation as already established and focuses on what published analyses must still make checkable for open science.
items.explain: The chapter bridges from Chapter 8’s toolkit to research-facing reuse requirements without repeating the full ops playbook.
